title: Example Selection Matters
concept: Diverse, representative, and correctly-labeled examples outperform random or redundant ones.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we learned about 'Few-Shot' prompting. How does it teach the model?

options.text: It uses strict math formulas to guide the model.
It shows the model a few worked examples of input and output.
It trains the model on millions of new web pages.

exercises.explain: Few-shot prompting provides the model with concrete input-output examples (demonstrations) to show it exactly how to format and solve the task.

cards.title: Garbage In, Garbage Out
cards.body: If you feed an AI three examples of short, happy emails, it might assume *every* email must be short and happy. Bad examples lead to bad AI assumptions! Let's learn how to choose them wisely.

cards.title: Curating Your Examples
cards.body: Example selection is the art of choosing which demonstrations to put in your few-shot prompt. To get the best results, your examples must be diverse, cover different scenarios, and be perfectly labeled.

cards.title: The Fruit Basket Analogy
cards.body: Imagine teaching a child to recognize fruits by only showing them three identical red apples. If they see a yellow banana or a green pear, they will get confused. You need to show them a diverse fruit basket instead!

cards.title: A Balanced Dataset
cards.body: Instead of using three positive reviews for a sentiment classifier, use one happy review, one angry review, and one mixed review. This teaches the model how to handle all kinds of real-world feedback.

cards.title: Quality Over Quantity
cards.body: Many developers think more examples are always better, even if they are repetitive or have typos. In reality, a few high-quality, diverse examples easily outperform dozens of messy, redundant ones.

cards.title: The Power of Variety
cards.body: Compare a repetitive, narrow set of examples with a diverse, well-distributed set of examples.

left.text: All examples are simple positive statements. The model fails on negative inputs.

right.text: Examples cover positive, negative, and mixed tones. The model handles anything.

exercises.prompt: If you build a sentiment classifier but your few-shot examples only show positive text, what will likely happen?

options.text: The model will refuse to answer.
The model will likely classify almost everything as positive.
The model will automatically detect negative sentiments anyway.
The prompt will crash the LLM.

exercises.explain: Using only positive examples creates a strong bias. The model is likely to classify neutral or negative inputs as positive because it hasn't seen any other pattern.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LLMs can easily ignore incorrect labels in your few-shot examples and still give the right answers.
exercises.explain: Label accuracy is critical. If you label a sad text as 'happy' in your examples, the model will copy your mistakes and perform poorly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule for selecting good prompt examples:
exercises.sentence: To get the best performance, your few-shot examples must be representative of the actual data the model will process.
exercises.explain: We want our prompt examples to be representative of the real-world data the model will actually encounter.

exercises.prompt: Identify the poorly designed, AI-generated few-shot example set:

options.text: An expert prompt containing one positive, one negative, and one neutral example.
A prompt using three identical positive reviews, all saying 'Great product!' with the same label.
A prompt testing a variety of short and long sentences with different emotional tones.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by AI because it shows a repetitive pattern of only positive, short reviews, which lacks the necessary diversity for a robust few-shot prompt.

exercises.prompt: Why do diverse, representative, and correctly-labeled examples produce far better LLM responses than three very similar examples?

options.text: They decrease response speed because the model has to process more tokens.
They prevent the model from forming narrow biases or repeating the same labels.
They teach the model new words that it has never encountered in its training.
They force the model to ignore the system instructions and only look at examples.

exercises.explain: Just like a human student, an AI needs to see the full picture. Selecting diverse examples that represent different types of inputs, and ensuring they are labeled correctly, prevents the LLM from making incorrect assumptions or getting stuck in a repetitive loop.
